checks.a_analysis: The output document's text accurately reflects the content of the source PDF. I reviewed all paragraphs and sentences in the JSON and found corresponding text in the source document across all 7 pages. There are no invented section headings or body text.
I verified all phone numbers listed in the output against page 3 of the source document. Every number matches exactly, and no extra numbers or contact details have been added.
All pages (1 through 7) and all numbered sections (1 through 13) are present in the output. The attendance tables, minutes text, financial summaries, and roll call vote results are all accounted for.

The tables in the JSON output faithfully reproduce the rows from the source document. The main attendance table on page 1, the roll call tables on pages 2 and 3, and the vote table on page 4 all contain the expected names and data points without significant row loss.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The content within each section is complete. No substantial key paragraphs or technical details are missing. The split of the sentence about 'cybersecurity assessment meetings' between two elements due to an image/page break is technically accurate and does not constitute omission.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The wording in the JSON output preserves the meaning of the source text. Numbers, dates, and technical terminology (e.g., fund balances, PSAP counts, meeting times) are consistent with the original document.

checks.a_analysis: The 'Roll Call Vote' table appears twice in the source document (once on page 4 and again on page 6). However, the output includes three image elements (indices 31, 33, and 35) representing this content. Element 35 is a redundant placeholder with no alt text, representing a third occurrence not justified by the source document structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: The 'Roll Call Vote' table appears on page 4 and page 6.

checks.a_analysis: The document title is correctly assigned to the top-level section title. While the document has a flat hierarchy where major section headings are represented as bolded paragraphs, this does not constitute a skip or incorrect nesting in the existing structural headers provided in the JSON.
The tables on pages 1, 2, 3, and 4/6 are accurately represented in the JSON structure. Columns and headers are correctly identified, including row spans for 'N/A' markers in the attendance tables.

There is a major disruption in the reading order. Section 12 (911 Regional Coordinator Reports) begins at element index 52 (at the bottom of page 6), but its concluding paragraph from the top of page 7 ('assessment meetings. She also is working...') is placed much earlier at element index 34, appearing before sections 6 through 11. This prevents a logical top-to-bottom reading of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 6 concludes section 12 with '...starting work on the cybersecurity'. Page 7 continues with 'assessment meetings. She also is working...'
checks.b_evidence_output: Element index 34 ('assessment meetings...') appears before element index 52 ('12. 911 Regional Coordinator Reports...').

checks.a_analysis: Several lists in the source are converted to flat paragraphs in the output. For example, the list of fund accounts in section 5 (c, d, e, f) and the 'Phone Numbers' list on page 3 are both rendered as single paragraphs, losing their list-based semantic structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 4: 'c) CMRS Fund/Account... d) PSAP Fund/Account...'; Page 3: list of Phone Numbers.

checks.a_analysis: No fabricated links were found. The document contains no active URLs in the source text.

checks.a_analysis: The list of guests in the 'Meeting Attendees' table is truncated. In the source PDF, the 'Guests via Teams' column starts on page 1 and continues onto the top of page 2 with six additional names (Ray Silance, Gathany Smith, Tracy Trogdon, Mark Whitby, Vic Williams, and Ashley Wilson). The HTML version of this table ends at 'Corrine Rust' (the end of page 1), omitting those six names from this organizational block. While the names appear in a later roll call table, their absence here breaks the visual flow and completeness of the attendee list.
checks.b_evidence_source: Ray Silance, Gathany Smith, Tracy Trogdon, Mark Whitby, Vic Williams, Ashley Wilson
checks.b_evidence_output: [The list ends at Corrine Rust followed immediately by 'Call to Order']

checks.a_analysis: The 'Roll Call - Guests' table in the HTML lacks the necessary column headers found in the source. In the PDF, this table is part of a continuous structure where columns like 'VIA MS TEAMS/PHONE' are established. In the HTML, it is rendered as a standalone table without these headers, making the 'X' marks in the third column visually ambiguous and harder to interpret for a sighted user.
checks.b_evidence_source: Table structure on page 3 continuing from page 2 headers 'VIA MS TEAMS/PHONE'
checks.b_evidence_output: [Table lacks column headers for the 'X' marks]

checks.a_analysis: The 'NC DIT' logo image is missing from the header of the 'Roll Call Vote' table in the HTML. In the source PDF (page 4), this logo is clearly present as part of the table's header block. While the logo appears in an earlier table in the HTML, its omission from this specific header is a visual discrepancy for a meaningful branding element.
checks.b_evidence_source: NC DIT logo in the ROLL CALL VOTE table header on page 4
checks.b_evidence_output: [Logo is absent from the 'Roll Call Vote for Consent Agenda...' table header]

checks.a_analysis: The 'ROLL CALL VOTE' table is duplicated on pages 4 and 6 of the source PDF. The HTML output correctly identifies this as redundant and renders the table only once, which is appropriate for a continuous document flow. No other genuine body content is duplicated.
